case_name: Zainab Abbas, M.D. v. Hetep Bilal Neter-Nu

summary.brief: Zainab Abbas, M.D. v. Hetep Bilal Neter-Nu. Medical malpractice case involving surgery orthopaedic.

evidence_index.text: Franklyn Neter-Nu was a truck driver who checked into the emergency room at Methodist Hospital in Gary, Indiana, on July 27, 2015, complaining of nausea and vomiting.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: He was given fluids and medications through an IV, but his attending nurse, Nurse Mittler, twice found the IV detached from Neter-Nu’s arm.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: She then placed the IV in Neter-Nu’s right foot, despite her lack of training and without a doctor’s order for that placement.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On July 28, Neter-Nu complained of pain in his right foot, so Nurse Mittler paged Dr. Abbas.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: An x-ray ordered for his foot showed no fracture or soft-tissue swelling.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On July 30, Dr. Abbas discharged Neter-Nu. The discharge note instructed Neter-Nu to return to the emergency room if symptoms persisted or worsened.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: He then checked into Mercy Medical Center emergency/urgent care with black toes where treating physicians found no blood flow in several of his right toes.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Concluding that the foot was “unsalvageable,” ER doctors referred Neter-Nu to the University of Nebraska Medical Center, where, on August 19, he underwent a below-the-knee amputation of his right leg.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: We hold that the trial court erred when it denied Methodist’s Trial Rule 50(A) motion for partial judgment on the evidence and when it calculated prejudgment interest.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Therefore, we affirm the jury verdict but reverse and remand with instructions to grant Methodist’s Rule 50(A) motion and recalculate prejudgment interest based on the Providers’ statutory liability.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: During cross-examination of Neter-Nu’s expert, Dr. Eric Tripp, the trial court denied Providers’ request to use certain emails to refresh the expert’s recollection or impeach him for inconsistent methodologies.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Ultimately, the jury returned an $11,000,000 verdict against all Providers, which the trial court reduced to the $1,250,000 statutory cap.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The trial court also awarded Neter-Nu $79,993.40 in prejudgment interest.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: In malpractice cases, like other kinds of negligence actions, the plaintiff must prove (1) the defendant owed the plaintiff a duty of care, (2) the defendant breached its duty by allowing its conduct to fall below the applicable standard of care, and (3) a compensable injury proximately caused by the defendant’s breach of duty.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Joint-and-several liability applies to negligent defendants in medical-malpractice suits.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Franklyn Neter-Nu presented to the emergency room at Methodist Hospital with complaints of nausea and vomiting.

focused_exam.general: Patient appeared unwell with nausea and vomiting
focused_exam.extremities: Pain in right foot noted on July 28

critical_findings: Pain in right foot after IV placement

requestables.name: X-ray of right foot
requestables.description: X-ray to assess for fracture or soft-tissue swelling

reveal.clinical_significance: Did not reveal cause of foot pain

timeline_phases.name: Emergency Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents to ED with nausea and vomiting

key_events.event: Patient discharged with instructions to return if symptoms worsen

timeline_phases.name: Discharge Decision
timeline_phases.description: Patient discharged with instructions to return if symptoms worsen

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient in ED with IV placed in right foot
decision_points.prompt: What should be done after noticing pain in the foot with IV infiltration?

options.description: Remove IV from foot and monitor for complications
options.clinical_reasoning: Standard care to prevent further complications from IV infiltration

options.description: Continue with IV in foot and discharge patient
options.clinical_reasoning: Inappropriate due to risk of complications from IV infiltration

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant continued with IV in foot and discharged patient

expected_action_court.description: Remove IV from foot and monitor for complications
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert testimony indicated this was the appropriate action

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to remove IV from foot led to complications
explanation.what_should_have_happened: IV should have been removed and foot monitored
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Standard of care as established by expert testimony

patient_outcome.description: Patient developed black toes and foot was deemed unsalvageable

key_findings: Breach of duty in failing to consult vascular surgery
Causation established - earlier intervention could have prevented loss of foot

malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Consultation could have identified vascular compromise

factual_timeline.event: Patient arrives at Methodist Hospital ED

diagnoses.diagnosis: Acute limb ischemia leading to amputation

treatments.outcome: Amputation performed due to unsalvageable foot

treatments.treatment: Referral to University of Nebraska Medical Center

allegations.allegation: Failure to properly manage IV infiltration
allegations.against: Dr. Zainab Abbas and Nurse Morgan Mittler

court_findings.legal_significance: Breach led to severe patient outcome
